author: @chris993361
text: I like it and it has lots of uses. I am not sure however that it is the best for turning something like wind into generator output. When the slide goes stationary during something like a break in the wind, the output cannot coast with inertia from something like a flywheel and instead comes to a stop or back drives the rack. That is one place a sprag or ratchet still has an advantage over the sliding gear. That being said, it appears to be a great device, and I may try and come up with a place to make use of it. Thanks for sharing as always!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i like the flywheel idea mate

author: @DaremoKamen
text: Is this leading to a lever based CVT?  Input goes to one end of a lever with a moving fulcrum then the other end of the lever goes to the input of the mechanical rectifier?

replies.text: lol - spot on mate - i am an open book!

replies.author: @DaremoKamen
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Actually, I'd been thinking of two wheels with a connecting rod where the attachment points could shift so that if it was near the hub on the input wheel it was near the rim on the output wheel and vice versa.  I realized it would either jam or the wheels would have the same rpm.  Then I was thinking a single wheel so the length of stroke would vary depending where the connection was, but needed some way to return the linear motion to rotary.  And then your video dropped.

author: @marcoviniciomorafallas1719
text:
The Torsen transmission is also a type of mechanical rectifier — but it doesn't use ratchets.
It redirects torque based on resistance and gear interaction, allowing rotation in both directions but distributing power only when there's a difference in load.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: hmmm - i am not sure about that mate - rectification is about allowing movement in one direction despite input from two directions - the two nature of a torson kind of precludes by definition it being considered as rectification doesn't it?

replies.author: @marcoviniciomorafallas1719
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering The Torsen differential is quite hard to understand, mainly because the relative movements between components make it difficult to visualise what's actually happening.

But if you imagine yourself rotating along with the whole system, you would see that when both output shafts rotate in the same direction, nothing really happens. However, when one shaft tries to rotate in the opposite direction, the system locks up.

This happens because a worm gear mechanism can transmit motion from the worm to the gear, but not from the gear to the worm — unless the worm has multiple threads and is specially designed to allow it.

That’s why, under certain conditions, the Torsen behaves like a mechanical rectifier: it resists differential motion in one direction, while allowing torque distribution under normal load.

author: @conkerfromopako
text: Been racking my brain trying to find the best way of building an entirely DIY-able wind power setup, with no magnets, field coils only, wonder if you've had a go at that kind of generator.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah i did some stuff on that a few years ago mate

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text:
@conkerfromopako Isn't that a catch 22 though? You need a magnetic field passing through the coils to generate electricity, and can't use induction like in a motor to create the field unless you already have electricity, so you're pretty much stuck with needing magnets.
As I don't like pointing at problems without offering some solution, potentially you could magnetize the rotor by using static electricity.

replies.author: @conkerfromopako
replies.text: @VeniceInventors Obviously you'd need some starting electric power to keep the coils on, but you can then switch to using the generator's own output after startup: thats how almost all generators in power plants work, as no permanent magnet survives for long the magnetic stress inside a big alternator; though in my case I'm just interested on simplifying the design
